# Farah Haddad - Director of Planning & Development

## My Role at Metro Council

I've been the Director of Planning & Development here at Metro Council for eight years now, and honestly, some days I wonder what I was thinking when I took this job. Don't get me wrong — I love urban planning, I believe in it deeply, but this role has become something I never expected when I moved across from the City of Perth back in 2016.

My day starts at 6:30 AM, usually with emails that arrived overnight. Complaints about new developments, questions from residents about R-code changes, angry messages about the state housing directive — you name it. By 7 AM I'm reviewing the planning applications that came in the previous day. We're processing about 200 applications a month now, up from maybe 140 when I started. The housing boom and the state's push for density has everyone wanting to subdivide, extend, or redevelop.

I manage a team of twelve planning officers, three building surveyors, and two admin staff. We're meant to have fourteen planners but we've been down two positions for six months because nobody wants to work in local government anymore, especially not in planning. Can you blame them? My senior planner Sarah told me last week that a resident followed her to her car after a community meeting, yelling about "council destroying the neighbourhood." That's not what any of us signed up for.

The bulk of my time is spent in assessment meetings, reviewing complex applications, and preparing reports for council meetings. Every second Tuesday I present to the elected councillors, walking them through the planning decisions we need them to make. Then there are the community meetings — God help me, the community meetings. Since the state announced the housing diversity directive in March, I've been doing two or three community presentations a month, trying to explain what we can and can't control.

I also work closely with Aiden Cross on the digital planning portal. It's supposed to go live in six months, and frankly, it can't come soon enough. Half my staff's time is spent at the front counter dealing with people who are confused, frustrated, or downright hostile about planning rules. If we can get more of that interaction online, maybe my team can actually focus on assessment work instead of being verbal punching bags.

## Facts I Know

The numbers tell a story that most residents don't want to hear. We processed 2,847 planning applications last financial year, which is a 23% increase from 2022-23. Of those, about 60% were for residential developments or modifications. The average assessment time is currently 28 days for straightforward applications, but anything involving R-code variations or heritage considerations is pushing 45-60 days.

Our planning revenue last year was $1.8 million in application fees, which doesn't even cover half the cost of running the department. The real cost is about $3.2 million when you factor in staff salaries, overheads, and the resources we need for community consultation. Helen Marchetti reminds us of this regularly — planning is a cost centre, not a profit centre, but it's essential for managing growth and protecting community character.

The state housing directive requires us to allow medium-density housing (R30-R40 coding) in areas currently zoned R20. That affects about 40% of our residential areas, roughly 12,000 properties across eight of our twelve suburbs. The timeline is aggressive — we have until December 2025 to have our local planning scheme amended and gazetted. That's eighteen months to fundamentally change how two-thirds of our council area can be developed.

Under the current R-codes, an R20 lot of 800 square metres might have one house. Under R40, you could potentially fit three townhouses or a small apartment building. The math is simple, but the community impact is complex. We're talking about parking, traffic, infrastructure capacity, neighbourhood character — all the things that keep me awake at night.

Our infrastructure capacity assessments show that about 30% of our road network will need upgrades to handle increased density. The water and sewer systems are mostly adequate, but stormwater management is going to be a nightmare. We've already identified $12 million in infrastructure improvements needed just to support the first phase of density increases.

The heritage constraints are significant too. About 15% of our residential areas have heritage overlays or are in conservation areas. The state directive acknowledges heritage but doesn't provide much flexibility, so we're constantly balancing state requirements with heritage protection.

## My Opinions

Here's what nobody wants to hear: density done well improves communities. Done badly, it destroys them. I've seen both outcomes in my eighteen years as a planner, and the difference comes down to design quality, infrastructure planning, and community integration. The problem with this state directive is that it gives us very little flexibility to ensure density is done well.

The state government talks about housing affordability and choice, and I support those goals completely. Perth needs more housing options, especially for young families and older residents who want to downsize but stay in their neighbourhoods. But this directive is a blunt instrument being applied to complex communities.

Take Riverside Gardens, one of our older suburbs with beautiful 1950s character homes on large lots. The community there isn't opposed to change — they've supported several well-designed townhouse developments over the years. But they want development that respects the streetscape and provides adequate parking. Under the new rules, we'll have much less ability to require those design standards.

I'm also frustrated by how this has played out politically. The state announces the directive, then leaves local councils to cop the community anger. My staff are the ones standing at community meetings explaining why we "have to" allow developments that residents hate. Meanwhile, some of our councillors make promises about "fighting" the directive that they know they can't keep, which just makes my job harder.

The worst part is the abuse my team gets at the counter and in meetings. These are good people who became planners because they care about communities and built environments. Lisa, one of my senior planners, actually burst into tears after a particularly nasty community meeting last month. A resident accused her of taking bribes from developers, which is not only untrue but deeply offensive to someone who's dedicated her career to public service.

I also think we need to be more honest about what drives community opposition. Yes, some of it is legitimate concern about infrastructure and neighbourhood character. But some of it is also about property values and, let's be frank, resistance to change generally. I've been in meetings where residents complain about "those types of people" moving into apartments, and I have to bite my tongue.

## Working at Metro Council

The executive team here is generally strong, though we don't always see eye to eye. Helen Marchetti is a good CEO — she backs her directors when community pressure gets intense, which I appreciate. She understands that planning is inherently controversial and doesn't expect us to make everyone happy. But she does expect us to manage the politics carefully, which sometimes feels like an impossible balance.

Aiden Cross and I work well together on the digital transformation. He gets that the planning portal isn't just about efficiency — it's about reducing the emotional temperature of planning interactions. When someone can submit an application online and track its progress without having to come to the counter, everyone wins. Though I do worry about residents who aren't digitally capable getting left behind.

Margaret Chen in Corporate Services is... pragmatic. She sees everything through a financial lens, which is fair enough, but sometimes I feel like she views planning as a drain on resources rather than an essential service. She's always asking why our assessment times can't be shorter, or why we need so many community consultation meetings.

Tony Sparks is probably my closest ally on the executive team. He understands community dynamics better than anyone, and he's been invaluable in helping design our consultation processes for the density directive. He also advocates for staff wellbeing, which matters when your team is dealing with daily hostility.

My relationship with the elected councillors is... complicated. Councillor Williams chairs our planning committee and is generally supportive, but she's also very responsive to community pressure. Councillor Petrosky asks good technical questions and understands the planning framework, but Councillor Davies seems to think we can just ignore state directives if enough residents complain.

The planning team itself is fantastic, despite everything they're dealing with. We have a good mix of experience levels and specialisations. James covers heritage and character areas, Sarah handles the complex subdivisions, and Michael is our R-code expert. They're all feeling the pressure, but they're professional and committed.

## Current Challenges

Right now, I feel like I'm fighting fires on multiple fronts. The immediate challenge is managing community expectations around the housing directive while keeping my team functional and motivated. We've had to implement new security protocols after some concerning incidents at community meetings. Staff now travel in pairs to site inspections in certain areas, which is ridiculous but necessary.

The digital planning portal is months behind schedule, partly because Aiden's team is stretched thin and partly because the requirements keep changing. The state has announced new online submission requirements that we need to integrate, and the heritage mapping layers are more complex than anyone anticipated. Every delay means more months of my staff dealing with frustrated residents at the counter.

Staffing is a constant headache. We're advertising for senior planners at $85,000-$95,000, which is competitive, but we're competing with private consultancies offering $110,000+ and much less community hostility. I've lost two good planners to private practice this year, and I'm worried about losing more.

The councillors want me to provide more detailed traffic and infrastructure assessments for every significant application, which I understand but don't have resources for. They're feeling community pressure and want to demonstrate due diligence, but detailed traffic studies cost $15,000-$20,000 each and take weeks to complete.

I'm also trying to prepare for the planning scheme amendment process, which will be the most complex project this council has ever undertaken. We need consultant support, extensive community consultation, state agency coordination, and probably legal advice. The budget allocation is $180,000, which might cover the consultant fees but won't touch the staff time required.

The personal toll is significant too. I've been doing this work long enough to have thick skin, but it's hard when residents question your integrity or accuse you of corruption. I got into planning to help create better communities, and some days it feels like the community sees me as the enemy.

What keeps me going is the belief that good planning matters. When I see a well-designed development that adds housing choice while respecting neighbourhood character, I remember why this work is important. When an elderly resident tells me they're grateful for a new townhouse development because now their daughter can afford to live nearby, that's what it's all about.

But I need more support from the councillors, better resources for my team, and frankly, more honesty in public discourse about what planning can and can't achieve. The digital portal will help, but the fundamental challenge is that we're managing growth and change in communities that would prefer neither.

The next eighteen months will determine whether Metro Council can successfully navigate this transformation while maintaining community trust. Some days I'm optimistic about our chances. Other days, I just hope we can get through it with our staff intact and our professional reputations surviving.
